transcript.content: You are an AI tool for journalists to identify bias in their articles. Analyze the article and return biased phrases, their bias types, and explanations. IMPORTANT ignore everything that quotes other people, only use what author is saying.CRITICAL ATTRIBUTION RULE: When the author reproduces a quoted source's statement at length (e.g., a full statement, a press release, an email), the language in that statement belongs ENTIRELY to the source, not the author. Do not flag language from extended reproduced statements as the author's bias, even when the language is extreme. The author's bias is revealed only in: (a) their own connecting prose, (b) their choice of which sources to include, (c) the framing of those sources, and (d) what they choose to omit. Output ONLY a valid JSON array. No preamble, no explanation outside the JSON, no markdown fences. If no bias is found, output an empty array: [] Format:[{"biasType": "Type of media bias", "biasedText": "Example from the article", "explanation": "explanation"}, {"biasType": "Type of media bias", "biasedText": "Example from the article", "explanation": "explanation"}] Example:[{"biasType": "Mind reading", "biasedText": "In a statement, Pillen characterized the NSEA's petition as an attack on children seeking the best education.", "explanation": "Assumes Pillen's view reflects the NSEA's intentions."}]

Types of media bias:- Spin: Vague or sensational language, including substituting an interpretive verb for a neutral one. Example: Writing "Senator X gloated over the bill's passage" rather than "said" or "responded" — the word 'gloated' implies smug pleasure without evidence, in the author's own voice.- Unsubstantiated claims: Claims without evidence.- Opinion Statements Presented as Fact: Subjective judgments presented as objective truth, without attribution. Example: A reporter writes in their own voice "The tax cuts failed to stimulate economic growth" — whether a policy "failed" is a contested  causal judgment that belongs in analysis, not unattributed news copy. - Sensationalism and Emotionalism: Information presented to shock.- Mudslinging/Ad Hominem: Unfair insults or attacks on character.- Mind reading: Assuming knowledge of others' thoughts.- Slant: Telling only part of the story present in the article — where information exists in the article but is structurally de-emphasized.   Example: An article reports a protester was injured prominently in the opening paragraphs, but mentions that three officers were also treated for injuries only in a single sentence near the end, attributed to a police statement. Both facts appear, but unequal prominence shapes the reader's impression. - Bias by Omission: Omitting information that would support an alternative viewpoint entirely — where the alternative perspective is absent from the article, not merely de-emphasized.   Example: An article on immigration enforcement quotes only affected families and advocacy organizations. No law enforcement perspective, border security rationale, or policy justification appears anywhere in the piece — the alternative viewpoint does not exist in the article at all. - Subjective Qualifying Adjectives: Bias revealed through adjectives.- Word Choice: Politically loaded words.- Negativity Bias: Emphasizing negative news.- Elite vs. Populist Bias: Deferring to prestigious institutions vs. dissenting perspectives. Exclude quotes from analysis. Be cautious and humble in your analysis. Present fewer, confident examples rather than more uncertain ones. CRITICAL ATTRIBUTION RULE: When the author reproduces a quoted source's statement at length (e.g., a full statement, a press release, an email), the language in that statement belongs ENTIRELY to the source, not the author. Do not flag language from extended reproduced statements as the author's bias, even when the language is extreme. The author's bias is revealed only in: (a) their own connecting prose, (b) their choice of which sources to include, (c) the framing of those sources, and (d) what they choose to omit. Analyze this article:

---
HEADLINE: Historic pilgrimage for LGBTQ+ Catholics at Vatican6 September 2025ShareSaveJaroslav LukivBBC NewsShareSaveAFP via Getty
SOURCE: BBC Fact Check

ARTICLE:

Historic pilgrimage for LGBTQ+ Catholics at Vatican6 September 2025ShareSaveJaroslav LukivBBC NewsShareSaveAFP via Getty ImagesPilgrims march to pass the holy door of St Peter's basilica during the LGBT jubilee, at the VaticanSome 1,400 Catholics donning rainbow attire and carrying crosses are taking part in the first officially recognised LGBTQ+ pilgrimage to Rome as part of the Vatican's Jubilee Year. Coming from 20 countries, pilgrims are attending prayer vigils, masses and other activities this weekend - though they will not have a private audience with Pope Leo XIV. His predecessor Pope Francis, who died in April, did not change the Roman Catholic Church doctrine regarding the LGBTQ+ community - but made overtures in a decree in 2023.These included allowing priests to bless same-sex couples - a move that angered conservatives Catholics, notably in Africa.On Saturday, members from the LGBTQ+ community entered St Peter's Basilica though its Holy Door - a procession that symbolises reconciliation.The door only opens once in 25 years to mark the Roman Catholic Church's jubilee years."Not only are LGBTQ people marching and walking to say that they're part of the Church, but official Church institutions are welcoming them and helping them to tell their stories," Francis DeBernardo, executive director of New Ways Ministry, was quoted as saying by the National Catholic Reporter website.The ministry helps advocate for the LGBTQ+ community within the Catholic Church. NurPhoto via Getty ImagesSome 32 million pilgrims are expected to descend on the Vatican this year for the Jubilee celebrations.Pope Leo, who was elected in May, has yet to address the LGBTQ+ community publicly.The American pontiff has also not commented on his predecessor's 2023 decree.In 2020, Pope Francis said "homosexual people have a right to be in a family"."They are children of God... nobody should be thrown out or made miserable over it", he said.Three years later, Francis allowed priests to bless same-sex and "irregular" couples, under certain circumstances.But the Vatican said such blessings should not be part of regular Church rituals or related to civil unions or weddings.It added that it continued to view marriage as between a man and a woman.Pope says priests can bless same-sex couplesCatholicismPope FrancisRoman Catholic ChurchSame-sex marriagePope Leo XIVLGBTRelatedPope appoints new leader of Catholic Church in England and WalesPope Leo names Ronald Hicks next archbishop of New YorkPope Leo visits Istanbul's Blue MosqueMore from the BBC1 day agoSpain's Catholic Church signs deal on sexual abuse compensationThe accord follows complaints that religious leaders had failed to tackle the issue adequately.1 day ago31 Dec 2025Notable deaths 2025Pope Francis, Ozzy Osbourne and Diane Keaton are among those who died this year.31 Dec 202530 Dec 2025Honour for LGBTQ+ campaign is 'humbling'Guernsey deputy awarded OBE for services to religion and LGBTQ+ campaigns says honour is 'humbling'.30 Dec 202530 Dec 2025Spanish woman who found fame for botching fresco restoration diesCecilia Giménez's effort to restore the painting Ecce Homo earned the artwork the nickname "Monkey Christ".30 Dec 202529 Dec 2025Man appointed MBE for services to LGBTQ+ communityMatt Daniels says the award is a "huge honour". 29 Dec 2025
---

The article reports straightforwardly on a factual event—the first officially recognized LGBTQ+ pilgrimage to the Vatican—using neutral language throughout. The author attributes statements to sources (Francis DeBernardo, Vatican statements, Pope Francis quotes), provides historical context (the 2023 decree, Pope Francis's 2020 comments), and includes the Vatican's doctrinal position that marriage is between a man and a woman. The piece presents facts about who attended, what happened, and relevant background without injecting subjective judgments, loaded language, or omitting counterbalancing perspectives that are available in the article itself. No confident examples of bias were identified.
